claims.text: Approximately 50% of business activity is devoted to creating the false illusion of certainty; the effort is misdirected because certainty cannot exist in the real world.
claims.speaker: Unknown speaker

claims.text: Big data is often misdirected effort to create a false sense of certainty, and the volume of data is not a guide to its importance or reliability because all big data comes from the past.
claims.speaker: Unknown speaker
claims.mechanism: Historical data cannot predict paradigm shifts; volume of historical data points does not improve prediction quality when underlying assumptions change.
bounded_by: Does not apply to real-time operational metrics where past patterns hold
Does not apply to forecasting within established market conditions

examples.text: Nokia had extensive data points indicating that the world's population would only spend a limited amount on a mobile phone. An anthropologist, Amy Chen, observed behavior in a poor region of China where smartphone adoption created entirely new spending patterns, invalidating Nokia's volumetric historical data. Nokia dismissed the observation because it was based on 100 data points versus their thousands, but failed to recognize that all their data came from the same past era and could not account for paradigm-shift changes in product category and consumer behavior.

practices.text: When evaluating data-driven claims, examine whether the data comes from the same era and conditions; question whether volume of data points is a reliable proxy for predictive power, especially at industry inflection points.
